original_abstract:
Hybrid porous nanowire arrays, consisting of strongly coupled $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$ and carbon, were synthesized via the straightforward carbonization of a metal-organic framework (MOF) grown directly on copper foil. This architecture yields a high surface area of $251\text{ m}^2\text{ g}^{-1}$ and a significant carbon content (52.1 wt%), allowing the material to serve as a self-supported binder-free electrode for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). The electrode demonstrates exceptional performance in alkaline media (0.1 and 1.0 M KOH), achieving a low onset potential of 1.47 V vs. RHE and maintaining a stable current density of $10.0\text{ mA cm}^{-2}$ at 1.52 V for over 30 hours with a Faradaic efficiency of 99.3%. 

This superior catalytic activity and durability, which outperform many state-of-the-art noble-metal and transition-metal catalysts, are attributed to the synergistic effects of the nanowire array configuration and in situ carbon incorporation. These features ensure a large active surface area, rapid mass and charge transport, efficient release of oxygen bubbles, and robust structural integrity. Additionally, the $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$–carbon hybrid exhibits bifunctional activity by effectively gauging the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) through a preferred four-electron pathway. This versatility positions the material as a promising candidate for rechargeable metal-air batteries, regenerative fuel cells, and other advanced clean energy technologies.
humanized_abstract:
The successful synthesis of hybrid porous nanowire arrays made from strongly coupled $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$ and carbon by heating the MOF precursors at 800 °C is reported. Such arrays possess high surface area (up to $251\text{ m}^2\text{ g}^{-1}$) and rich carbon content (up to 52.1 wt%), making them suitable as a self-supporting binder-free electrode for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). As demonstrated in both 0.1 and 1.0 M KOH solutions, the electrode shows a low onset potential of 1.47 V vs. RHE and a remarkable durability with a stable current density of $10.0\text{ mA cm}^{-2}$ at 1.52 V for over 30 hours with a Faradaic efficiency of 99.3%.

Oxide nanowire materials with enhanced performance have been obtained for fuel cell application. The synthesis route described here allows for the production of nanowire arrays of particular crystallographic orientation with intrinsic catalytic properties and resistance to corrosion superior to many existing noble and transition metal catalysts. The exceptional catalytic performance and stability of these oxide nanowire arrays are owing to a synergetic combination of nanostructure and in situ formed carbon. Indeed, such materials enable access to a large surface area, facile transport of reactants and products, an easy detachment of the dissolved oxygen produced at the catalyst surface as well as mechanical strength. The $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$–carbon hybrid material is shown to catalyze the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) via a favored four-electron process, thus opening routes for rechargeable battery technology of metal-air batteries and for different clean energy applications such as regenerative fuel cells.

Hybrid porous nanowire arrays, consisting of strongly coupled $\text{Co}_3\text{O}_4$ and carbon, were synthesized via the straightforward carbonization of a metal-organic framework (MOF) grown directly on copper foil.
